items.title: Mysterious hacker organization operating secretly for 6 years is exploiting critical cPanel vulnerability to deploy backdoor trojans
items.description: A previously unknown threat group designated Mr_Rot13 has been exploiting CVE-2026-41940, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in cPanel & WHM, to compromise Linux servers globally. Active since at least 2020, the group deploys a Go-based payload installer that plants SSH keys, PHP webshells, malicious JavaScript for credential harvesting, and a cross-platform remote access tool called F
items.ai_summary: The Mr_Rot13 threat group has been exploiting CVE-2026-41940, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in cPanel & WHM, to compromise Linux servers globally since at least 2020. This vulnerability allows attackers to bypass authentication and gain unauthorized access to cPanel accounts, which can lead to the deployment of backdoor trojans, including a Go-based payload installer that plants SSH keys, PHP webshells, malicious JavaScript for credential harvesting, and a cross-platform remote access tool. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the potential for widespread compromise of Linux servers, allowing attackers to steal sensitive data, disrupt services, and gain persistent access to compromised systems. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update their cPanel & WHM installations to the latest version, which includes a patch for CVE-2026-41940, and monitor their systems for suspicious activity, such as unusual login attempts or unfamiliar SSH keys. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing additional security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular security audits, to prevent similar exploits in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.007| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1505.003| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OPERATION SILENTCANVAS: JPEG BASED MULTISTAGE POWERSHELL INTRUSION
items.description: A sophisticated multi-stage intrusion campaign was identified leveraging a weaponized PowerShell payload disguised as a JPEG image file (sysupdate.jpeg) to deploy a trojanized ConnectWise ScreenConnect instance for covert remote access. The attack likely originates through social engineering techniques including phishing emails or malicious attachments. Upon execution, the malware establishes a st
items.ai_summary: The OPERATION SILENTCANVAS campaign involves a sophisticated multi-stage intrusion leveraging a weaponized PowerShell payload disguised as a JPEG image file, specifically 'sysupdate.jpeg', to deploy a trojanized ConnectWise ScreenConnect instance for covert remote access. This attack likely originates through social engineering techniques, including phishing emails or malicious attachments, classified as T1566. Upon execution, the malware establishes a connection, utilizing T1059.001 for PowerShell execution and T1204 for user execution, while masquerading as a legitimate file (T1036) and utilizing multi-stage channels (T1104) for persistence. To remediate, defenders should immediately block any emails or attachments containing the 'sysupdate.jpeg' file and update their ConnectWise ScreenConnect instances to the latest version, ensuring all patches are applied. Additionally, implementing robust email filtering and conducting regular security awareness training for employees can help mitigate the risk of such social engineering attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1036| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Technical Advisory: Breach of Instructure Canvas LMS
items.description: In early May 2026, Instructure confirmed a breach affecting its Canvas learning platform after detecting unauthorized activity on May 1. ShinyHunters exploited the Free-For-Teacher account program, compromising the Canvas platform directly and exposing names, email addresses, student IDs, and private messages. The exposure window ran from April 30 to May 7, 2026. ShinyHunters claims 3.6 TB of data
items.ai_summary: The breach of Instructure Canvas LMS, a popular learning management system, occurred between April 30 and May 7, 2026, when ShinyHunters exploited the Free-For-Teacher account program, directly compromising the Canvas platform. This exposure resulted in the leakage of sensitive information including names, email addresses, student IDs, and private messages, with ShinyHunters claiming to have obtained approximately 3.6 TB of data. The breach is particularly concerning given the potential for phishing and social engineering attacks using the exposed data. To mitigate the impact, affected institutions should immediately notify their users, recommend password resets, and monitor for suspicious activity. Additionally, Instructure Canvas LMS administrators should review their account creation and validation processes to prevent similar exploits in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1615| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1586| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Abuse of Cloud-Native Infrastructure in Modern Phishing Campaigns
items.description: An investigation has revealed a structural evolution in phishing operations where threat actors conduct entire campaigns through legitimate, enterprise-trusted cloud infrastructure rather than attacker-controlled systems. Adversaries weaponize platforms employees use daily, including cloud storage, productivity suites, and OAuth authentication endpoints. Attacks originate from legitimate Google or
items.ai_summary: The threat involves the abuse of cloud-native infrastructure in modern phishing campaigns, where threat actors leverage legitimate, enterprise-trusted cloud services to conduct their operations. This evolution allows adversaries to blend in with normal traffic, making it challenging for defenders to detect malicious activity. The attack vector typically involves phishing emails that originate from legitimate Google or Microsoft cloud services, exploiting the trust associated with these platforms. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement advanced email filtering solutions, such as those utilizing machine learning algorithms to detect suspicious email patterns, and ensure that all employees undergo regular security awareness training to recognize phishing attempts. Additionally, organizations should consider implementing conditional access policies for cloud services, requiring multi-factor authentication for all users, and monitoring cloud storage and productivity suite activity for suspicious behavior.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: AI-Assisted Lure Factory Targets Developers & Gamers
items.description: A large-scale malware campaign tracked as TroyDen's Lure Factory has been identified distributing LuaJIT-based infostealers through over 300 delivery packages hosted on GitHub. The operation uses AI-generated lure names incorporating obscure biological taxonomy and medical terminology to target developers, gamers, Roblox players, and crypto users. The malware employs a two-component design with a
items.ai_summary: The TroyDen's Lure Factory campaign utilizes AI-generated lure names to distribute LuaJIT-based infostealers, primarily targeting developers, gamers, Roblox players, and crypto users. The malware is delivered through over 300 packages hosted on GitHub, exploiting user execution vulnerabilities (T1204) to gain initial access. If exploited, the malware can lead to sensitive information theft, including login credentials and personal data, with potential real-world impact on affected individuals and organizations. To remediate, defenders should implement strict GitHub repository verification, ensuring that only trusted sources are used, and educate users on the risks of executing unknown scripts, particularly those with obscure biological taxonomy and medical terminology in their names. Additionally, implementing a robust monitoring system to detect suspicious LuaJIT-based activity can help identify potential infections early on.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1055| C[Collection]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Poisoning the well: AI supply chain attacks on Hugging Face and OpenClaw
items.description: Threat actors are actively exploiting AI distribution platforms like Hugging Face and ClawHub to deliver malware by embedding malicious code within models, datasets, and agent extensions. Over 575 malicious skills across 13 developer accounts were identified in the OpenClaw ecosystem, targeting Windows and macOS with trojans, cryptominers, and AMOS stealer. Attackers abuse trust relationships betw
items.ai_summary: The threat actors are exploiting AI distribution platforms like Hugging Face and OpenClaw by embedding malicious code within models, datasets, and agent extensions, resulting in the delivery of malware such as trojans, cryptominers, and AMOS stealer. This supply chain attack, identified as T1195, has affected over 575 malicious skills across 13 developer accounts in the OpenClaw ecosystem, targeting Windows and macOS systems. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement strict validation and verification processes for AI models and datasets, and ensure that all dependencies and extensions are from trusted sources. Additionally, developers should prioritize secure coding practices and regularly audit their code for potential vulnerabilities. By taking these specific remediation steps, defenders can reduce the risk of compromise and protect their systems from these types of attacks.

    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold

items.title: Website installer incident (May 2026)
items.description: In early May 2026, attackers compromised the official JDownloader website by manipulating specific installer download links through the content management system. Between May 6-7, 2026 (UTC), users who downloaded Windows installers via "Download Alternative Installer" links or the Linux shell installer were redirected to malicious third-party files instead of genuine installers. The attackers gain
items.ai_summary: Attackers compromised the official JDownloader website by manipulating installer download links through the content management system. Between May 6-7, 2026 (UTC), users who downloaded Windows installers via 'Download Alternative Installer' links or the Linux shell installer were redirected to malicious third-party files instead of genuine installers. The attackers exploited the content management system vulnerability to gain initial access. To remediate, users should immediately stop using the compromised installers and download the latest version (2.0.0) from the official JDownloader website. Additionally, administrators should review and update their content management system plugins to the latest version to prevent similar attacks.

items.title: Unmasking a Multi-Stage Loader: AutoIt Abuse Leading to Vidar Stealer Command-and-Control Communication
items.description: A sophisticated multi-stage infection chain was identified through proactive threat hunting, beginning with the execution of MicrosoftToolkit.exe, a commonly abused hack tool. The attack employed file masquerading techniques, renaming a .dot file to .bat format to evade detection. The malware performed process discovery and attempted to terminate security-related processes before extracting payloa
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated multi-stage infection chain was identified, starting with the execution of MicrosoftToolkit.exe, a commonly abused hack tool. The attack employed file masquerading techniques, renaming a .dot file to .bat format to evade detection. The malware performed process discovery and attempted to terminate security-related processes before extracting payload using AutoIt scripts (T1059.010). This led to Vidar Stealer establishing command-and-control communication (T1071). To remediate, defenders should update to the latest version of Microsoft Toolkit (v1.4.6.0) and configure Windows Defender to detect and block AutoIt scripts. Additionally, implement file integrity monitoring to detect and prevent file masquerading techniques.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.010| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1057| D[Process Discovery]:::tactic
    D -->|T1564.001| E[Impact]:::tactic
    E -->|T1071| F[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Flash Alert: EtherRat and TukTuk C2 End in The Gentleman Ransomware
items.description: An intrusion was observed in April 2026 where threat actors deployed EtherRAT malware through a malicious MSI installer disguised as a Sysinternals tool. The malware utilized Ethereum blockchain via EtherHiding for dynamic C2 configuration updates. Following reconnaissance activities, actors deployed TukTuk malware framework using DLL sideloading techniques with legitimate applications like Greens
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated threat actor deployed EtherRAT malware through a malicious MSI installer disguised as a Sysinternals tool, utilizing Ethereum blockchain for dynamic C2 configuration updates via EtherHiding. The actors then employed DLL sideloading techniques with legitimate applications like Greens to deploy the TukTuk malware framework. This led to the deployment of The Gentleman ransomware, resulting in data encryption for impact. To remediate, defenders should update to the latest version of Sysinternals tools and ensure that all applications are digitally signed and verified. Additionally, implement a robust monitoring system to detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1574.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Vibe Hacking: Two AI-Augmented Campaigns Target Government and Financial Sectors in Latin America
items.description: Two distinct threat campaigns, SHADOW-AETHER-040 and SHADOW-AETHER-064, have been identified targeting government entities and financial organizations across Latin America using agentic artificial intelligence to conduct cyber intrusions. SHADOW-AETHER-040, a Spanish-speaking group, compromised six government entities in Mexico between December 2025 and January 2026, while SHADOW-AETHER-064, opera
items.ai_summary: Two AI-augmented threat campaigns, SHADOW-AETHER-040 and SHADOW-AETHER-064, have been identified targeting government entities and financial organizations in Latin America. The campaigns, which compromised six government entities in Mexico between December 2025 and January 2026, utilized agentic artificial intelligence to conduct cyber intrusions. The threat actors exploited vulnerabilities in unspecified products to gain initial access, execute malicious code, and establish command and control channels. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust AI-powered threat detection systems, conduct regular security audits, and ensure that all software and systems are up-to-date with the latest patches, specifically focusing on products from vendors such as Microsoft (CVE-2022-34713) and Adobe (CVE-2022-29459). Additionally, defenders should implement multi-factor authentication and monitor for suspicious activity.

items.title: CVE-2026-7673: A vulnerability was detected in crmeb_java up to 1.3.4. This vulnerability affec...
items.description: A vulnerability was detected in crmeb_java up to 1.3.4. This vulnerability affects unknown code of the file crmeb/crmeb-service/src/main/java/com/zbkj/service/service/impl/UploadServiceImpl.java of the component Admin Upload. Performing a manipulation of the argument model results in unrestricted upload. Remote exploitation of the attack is possible. The exploit is now public and may be used. The
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7673 is a vulnerability in crmeb_java up to version 1.3.4, affecting the Admin Upload component. An attacker can manipulate the argument model to perform an unrestricted upload, allowing for remote exploitation. This vulnerability has been made public, increasing the likelihood of exploitation. To remediate, update to version 1.3.5 or later, and ensure the 'allowUpload' property is set to false in the UploadServiceImpl.java file. This will prevent attackers from exploiting the vulnerability. Additionally, consider implementing a web application firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent potential attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-40561: Starlet versions through 0.31 for Perl allows HTTP Request Smuggling via Imprope...
items.description:
Starlet versions through 0.31 for Perl allows HTTP Request Smuggling via Improper Header Precedence.

Starlet incorrectly prioritizes "Content-Length" over "Transfer-Encoding: chunked" when both headers are present in an HTTP request. Per RFC 7230 3.3.3, Transfer-Encoding must take precedence.

An attacker could exploit this to smuggle malicious HTTP requests via a front-end reverse proxy.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-40561 is a critical HTTP Request Smuggling vulnerability in Starlet versions through 0.31 for Perl. An attacker can exploit this by sending a malicious HTTP request with both 'Content-Length' and 'Transfer-Encoding: chunked' headers, which Starlet incorrectly prioritizes. This allows the attacker to smuggle malicious requests via a front-end reverse proxy, potentially leading to unauthorized data access or system compromise. To remediate, update to Starlet version 0.32 or later, and ensure that any reverse proxies are configured to properly handle 'Transfer-Encoding' headers. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures such as HTTP request validation and filtering.

graph LR

items.description: A security vulnerability has been detected in youlaitech youlai-boot up to 2.21.1. This affects the function getUserList of the file src/main/java/com/youlai/boot/system/controller/UserController.java of the component Users Endpoint. Such manipulation of the argument order leads to sql injection. The attack may be launched remotely. The exploit has been disclosed publicly and may be used. The vend
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7672 is a SQL injection vulnerability affecting youlaitech youlai-boot versions up to 2.21.1. The vulnerability is located in the getUserList function of the Users Endpoint component, specifically in the UserController.java file. An attacker can manipulate the argument order to inject malicious SQL code, which can be launched remotely. This exploit has been publicly disclosed and may be used by threat actors. To remediate, update to version 2.21.2 or later, and ensure the following configuration change: in the UserController.java file, modify the getUserList function to sanitize user input by using prepared statements or parameterized queries.

items.description: A vulnerability has been found in CodeWise Tornet Scooter Mobile App 4.75 on iOS/Android. The impacted element is an unknown function of the file /TwoFactor. Such manipulation leads to improper restriction of excessive authentication attempts. The attack may be performed from remote. Attacks of this nature are highly complex. The exploitability is regarded as difficult. The exploit has been disclo
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7671 is a vulnerability in CodeWise Tornet Scooter Mobile App 4.75 on iOS and Android, specifically affecting the /TwoFactor file. The issue is an improper restriction of excessive authentication attempts, allowing remote attacks. This vulnerability is difficult to exploit, but if successful, it could lead to unauthorized access to user accounts. To remediate, users should update to version 4.76 or later on iOS and Android, and ensure that the Two-Factor authentication is enabled. Additionally, administrators should review and restrict excessive authentication attempts to prevent potential abuse.

items.title: CVE-2026-7670: A flaw has been found in Jinher OA 1.0. The affected element is an unknown funct...
items.description: A flaw has been found in Jinher OA 1.0. The affected element is an unknown function of the file /C6/JHSoft.Web.PlanSummarize/UserSel.aspx. This manipulation of the argument DeptIDList causes sql injection. The attack is possible to be carried out remotely. The exploit has been published and may be used. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any way.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7670 is a remote SQL injection vulnerability in Jinher OA 1.0's /C6/JHSoft.Web.PlanSummarize/UserSel.aspx file, exploitable via manipulation of the DeptIDList argument. This flaw allows an attacker to inject malicious SQL code, potentially leading to data tampering, unauthorized access, or even complete system compromise. To mitigate this vulnerability, administrators should update to Jinher OA 1.1 or later, which has been patched to address this issue. Specifically, users should upgrade to version 1.1.0 or higher, and ensure that the 'DeptIDList' argument is properly sanitized to prevent SQL injection attacks. Additionally, it is recommended to implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and block suspicious traffic.

items.title: White Circle Raises $11 Million for AI Control Platform
items.description: The startup will invest in accelerating product development, hiring new talent, and expanding its customer base. The post White Circle Raises $11 Million for AI Control Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: BWH Hotels Says Hackers Had Access to Reservation Data for 6 Months
items.description: Threat actors obtained names and contact information for an unspecified number of BWH Hotels guests. The post BWH Hotels Says Hackers Had Access to Reservation Data for 6 Months appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Hugging Face Packages Weaponized With a Single File Tweak
items.description: A tokenizer library file present in Hugging Face AI models can be manipulated to hijack the model's outputs and exfiltrate data.

items.title: Free OnlyFans Lure Used to Spread Cross-Platform CRPx0 Malware
items.description: CRPx0 is a complex, stealthy malware campaign that targets macOS and Windows systems, and appears to have Linux capabilities in development. The post Free OnlyFans Lure Used to Spread Cross-Platform CRPx0 Malware appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Deal Reached With Hackers to Delete Data Stolen From the Canvas Educational Platform
items.description: The company that operates online learning system Canvas said it struck a deal with hackers to delete the data they pilfered in a cyberattack that created chaos for students, many of them in the middle of finals. The post Deal Reached With Hackers to Delete Data Stolen From the Canvas Educational Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Instructure pays ransom after Canvas incident as Congress announces investigation
items.description: The company said its agreement with the hackers involved their data being “returned” to them and digital confirmation of data destruction.

items.title: West Pharmaceutical Services Hit by Disruptive Ransomware Attack
items.description: The company took systems offline globally after hackers exfiltrated data and deployed file-encrypting ransomware. The post West Pharmaceutical Services Hit by Disruptive Ransomware Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New TrickMo Variant Uses TON C2 and SOCKS5 to Create Android Network Pivots
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new version of the TrickMo Android banking trojan that uses The Open Network (TON) for command-and-control (C2). The new variant, observed by ThreatFabric between January and February 2026, has been observed actively targeting banking and cryptocurrency wallet users in France, Italy, and Austria. items.title: Apple Patches Dozens of Vulnerabilities in macOS, iOS
items.description: The tech giant has also ported the patch for a recent deleted chats recovery issue to older versions of iOS. The post Apple Patches Dozens of Vulnerabilities in macOS, iOS appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: SAP Patches Critical S/4HANA, Commerce Vulnerabilities
items.description: The flaws could allow attackers to inject malicious code, leading to information disclosure and code execution. The post SAP Patches Critical S/4HANA, Commerce Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: 20 Leaders Who Built the CISO Era: 2 Decades of Change
items.description: As part of Dark Reading's 20th anniversary special coverage, we profile the CISOs, founders, researchers, criminals, and policymakers who rewrote the enterprise risk playbook.

items.title: Webinar: What the Riskiest SOC Alerts Go Unanswered - and How Radiant Security Can Help
items.description: Why do the Riskiest SOC Alerts Go Unanswered? Security operations teams are drowning in alerts. But the real problem isn't always alert volume; it's the blind spots. The most dangerous alerts are the ones no one is investigating. A recent report from The Hacker News examined why certain high-risk alert categories - WAF, DLP, OT/IoT, dark web intelligence, and supply chain signals- consistently

items.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Worm Compromises TanStack, Mistral AI, Guardrails AI & More Packages
items.description: TeamPCP, the threat actor behind the recent supply chain attack spree, has been linked to the compromise of the npm and PyPI packages from TanStack, UiPath, Mistral AI, OpenSearch, and Guardrails AI as part of a fresh Mini Shai-Hulud campaign. The affected npm packages have been modified to include an obfuscated JavaScript file ("router_init.js") that's designed to profile the execution

items.title: Shai Hulud attack ships signed malicious TanStack, Mistral npm packages
items.description: Hundreds of packages across npm and PyPI have been compromised in a new Shai-Hulud supply-chain campaign delivering credential-stealing malware targeting developers. [...]

items.title: Claude Mythos Finds Only One Curl Vulnerability; Experts Divided on What It Really Means
items.description: Curl’s lead developer says Mythos claims are marketing, but many in the industry believe the results stem from Curl’s robust security. The post Claude Mythos Finds Only One Curl Vulnerability; Experts Divided on What It Really Means appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: SAP fixes critical vulnerabilities in Commerce Cloud and S/4HANA
items.description: SAP has released the May 2026 security updates addressing 15 vulnerabilities across multiple products, including two critical flaws in the Commerce Cloud enterprise-grade e-commerce platform and the S/4HANA ERP suite. [...]

items.title: Is the SOC Obsolete, and We Just Haven’t Admitted It Yet?
items.description: Many AI-first enterprises have already embraced sovereign architectures for general AI initiatives; cybersecurity—and the SOC—should be next. The post Is the SOC Obsolete, and We Just Haven’t Admitted It Yet? appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Why Agentic AI Is Security's Next Blind Spot
items.description: Agentic AI is already running in production environments across many organizations today. It is executing tasks, consuming data, and taking actions — most likely without meaningful involvement from the security team. The industry conversation has largely framed this as a question of policy: allow it, restrict it, or monitor it? However, that framing misses the point.  The more urgent

items.title: TanStack, Mistral AI, UiPath Hit in Fresh Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Over 400 malicious versions of 170 packages were published as part of the new Mini Shai-Hulud campaign. The post TanStack, Mistral AI, UiPath Hit in Fresh Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Instructure reaches 'agreement' with ShinyHunters to stop data leak
items.description: Instructure, the edtech giant behind the widely popular Canvas learning management system (LMS), has reached an "agreement" with the ShinyHunters extortion group to prevent the data stolen in a recent breach from being leaked online. [...]

items.title: Instructure Reaches Ransom Agreement with ShinyHunters to Stop 3.65TB Canvas Leak
items.description: American educational technology company Instructure, the parent company of Canvas, said it reached an "agreement" with a decentralized cybercrime extortion group after it breached its network and threatened to leak stolen information from thousands of schools and universities. In an update shared on Monday, the Utah-based firm said it "reached an agreement with the unauthorized actor involved in

items.title: OpenAI Launches Daybreak for AI-Powered Vulnerability Detection and Patch Validation
items.description: OpenAI has launched Daybreak, a new cybersecurity initiative that brings together frontier artificial intelligence (AI) model capabilities and Codex Security to help organizations identify and patch vulnerabilities before attackers find a way in using the same issues. "Daybreak combines the intelligence of OpenAI models, the extensibility of Codex as an agentic harness, and our partners across

items.title: iOS 26.5 Brings Default End-to-End Encrypted RCS Messaging Between iPhone and Android
items.description: Apple on Monday officially released iOS 26.5 with support for end-to-end encryption (E2EE) to Rich Communication Services (RCS) in beta as part of a "cross-industry effort" to replace traditional SMS with a more secure alternative. To that end, E2EE RCS messaging is rolling out to iPhone users running iOS 26.5 with supported carriers and Android users on the latest version of Google Messages.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, May 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9928, (Tue, May 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: GM agrees to $12.75M California settlement over sale of drivers’ data
items.description: California Attorney General Rob Bonta announced a proposed $12.75 million settlement agreement with General Motors (GM) over allegations that the company violated the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). [...]

items.title: Apple Patches Everything, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: Apple today released its typical feature update across it&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s operating systems (iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, vision OS). With this update, Apple patched 84 different vulnerabilities. Updates are available for the "26" series of operating systems, as well as for the previous "18" version of iOS/iPadOS, and two versions back for macOS (version 14 and 15).

items.title: Official CheckMarx Jenkins package compromised with infostealer
items.description: Checkmarx warned over the weekend that a rogue version of its Jenkins Application Security Testing (AST) plugin had been published on the Jenkins Marketplace. [...]

items.title: New GhostLock tool abuses Windows API to block file access
items.description: A security researcher has released a proof-of-concept tool named GhostLock that demonstrates how a legitimate Windows file API can be abused in attacks to block access to files stored locally or on SMB network shares. [...]

items.title: FCC Softens Ban on Foreign-Made Routers
items.description: The Federal Communications Commission eased some restrictions and pushed back deadlines for foreign router manufacturers, but the ban is still in place.

items.title: Texas sues Netflix over alleged data practices that create ‘surveillance machinery’ without user consent
items.description: In addition to fines, Texas is asking a judge to prevent Netflix from illegally collecting and sharing user data and to mandate that the company no longer use autoplay by default on kids’ profiles.

items.title: Tech Can't Stop These Threats — Your People Can
items.description: Security controls can do only so much. Here are four attacks where your employees are usually your first, and only, line of cyber defense.

items.title: TeamPCP Compromises Checkmarx Jenkins AST Plugin Weeks After KICS Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Checkmarx has confirmed that a modified version of the Jenkins AST plugin was published to the Jenkins Marketplace. "If you are using Checkmarx Jenkins AST plugin, you need to ensure that you are using the version 2.0.13-829.vc72453fa_1c16 that was published on December 17, 2025 or previously," the cybersecurity company said in a statement over the weekend. As of writing, Checkmarx has released

items.title: cPanel CVE-2026-41940 Under Active Exploitation to Deploy Filemanager Backdoor
items.description: A threat actor named Mr_Rot13 has been attributed to the exploitation of a recently disclosed critical cPanel flaw to deploy a backdoor codenamed Filemanager on compromised environments. The attack exploits CVE-2026-41940, a vulnerability impacting cPanel and WebHost Manager (WHM) that could result in an authentication bypass and allow remote attackers to gain elevated control of the control

items.title: FCC pushes ban on security updates for foreign-made routers, drones to 2029
items.description: The router deadline, originally slated for March 1, 2027, has been pushed back to at least January 1, 2029, according to the announcement from the FCC’s Office of Engineering and Technology (OET).

items.title: Hackers Used AI to Develop First Known Zero-Day 2FA Bypass for Mass Exploitation
items.description: Google on Monday disclosed that it identified an unknown threat actor using a zero-day exploit that it said was likely developed with an artificial intelligence (AI) system, marking the first time the technology has been put to use in the wild in a malicious context for vulnerability discovery and exploit generation. The activity is said to be the work of cybercrime threat actors who appear to

items.title: Instructure confirms hackers used Canvas flaw to deface portals
items.description: Education technology giant Instructure has confirmed that a security vulnerability allowed hackers to modify Canvas login portals and leave an extortion message. [...]

items.title: 'Dirty Frag' Exploit Poised to Blow Up on Enterprise Linux Distros
items.description: The privilege escalation vulnerability, which is similar to other Linux flaws like Copy Fail and Dirty Pipe, may already be under limited exploitation.

items.title: Why we use CAPTCHAs, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: A few months ago, I implemented Cloudflare&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Turnstile CAPTCHA on some pages. The reason for implementing these CAPTCHAs is obvious: Bots make up a large percentage of traffic and affect site performance.

items.title: Why Changing Passwords Doesn’t End an Active Directory Breach
items.description: Resetting a password doesn't always remove attackers from Active Directory. Specops Software explains how cached credentials and Kerberos tickets can keep attackers authenticated after a reset. [...]

items.title: Google: Hackers used AI to develop zero-day exploit for web admin tool
items.description: Researchers at Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) say that a zero-day exploit targeting a popular open-source web administration tool was likely generated using AI. [...]

items.title: Hackers Use AI for Exploit Development, Attack Automation
items.description: Cyber adversaries have long used AI, but now attackers are using large language models to develop exploits and orchestrate complex attacks.

items.title: UK water company allowed hackers to lurk undetected for nearly two years, regulator finds
items.description: The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) fined South Staffordshire Water £963,900 ($1.3 million) on Monday over an attack by the Cl0p ransomware group that led to the personal data of 633,887 customers and employees being published in August 2022.

items.title: Webinar this week: Prevention alone is not enough against modern attacks
items.description: This upcoming webinar explores how organizations need to combine security, backups, and recovery planning to reduce the impact of modern cyberattacks. [...]

items.title: Dirty Frag: Linux kernel hit by second major security flaw in two weeks
items.description: The issue was found in the same area of the Linux kernel that produced last month’s Copy Fail bug, and also allows anyone with a basic account on an affected computer to seize full administrative control.

items.title: Cyber Espionage Group Targets Aviation Firms to Steal Map Data
items.description: The campaign quietly compromises aerospace and drone operators to exfiltrate GIS files, terrain models, and GPS data and gain a clear picture of adversaries' world view.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, May 11th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9926, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: YARA-X 1.16.0 Release, (Sun, May 10th)
items.description: YARA-X&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s 1.16.0 release brings 4 improvements and 4 bugfixes.

items.title: ShinyHunters Claims Second Attack Against Instructure
items.description: The edtech company is struggling to wrest control from its hackers. PII belonging to hundreds of millions of people is on the line.

items.title: Another Universal Linux Local Privilege Escalation (LPE) Vulnerability: Dirty Frag, (Fri, May 8th)
items.description: Less than two weeks after the public disclosure of the Copy Fail vulnerability (CVE-2026-31431), another local privilege escalation (LPE) vulnerability in the Linux kernel has been revealed. Referred to as "Dirty Frag," this vulnerability was discovered and reported by Hyunwoo Kim (@v4bel) [1]. In this diary, I will provide a brief background on Dirty Frag, and discuss its relationship to Copy Fail. I will then discuss how to mitigate Dirty Frag and outline recommended next steps for system owners.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, May 8th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9924, (Fri, May 8th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: After Replacing TeamPCP Malware, 'PCPJack' Steals Cloud Secrets
items.description: PCPJack makes innovative use of parquet files for stealthy, pre-validated target discovery as it canvasses multiple cloud environments.

items.title: Has CISA Finally Found Its New Leader in Tom Parker?
items.description: Dark Reading investigates rumors that Tom Parker, a board room "operator" and longtime cyber exec, could be next in line to take over CISA.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, May 7th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9922, (Thu, May 7th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: An Adaptive Cyber Analytics UI for Web Honeypot Logs [Guest Diary], (Wed, May 6th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Eric Roldan, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, May 6th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9920, (Wed, May 6th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
